topic.what: Comprehensive review of autoimmune atrophic gastritis covering pathogenesis, clinical manifestations, diagnostic workup, and management strategies
topic.how: Expert synthesis of literature on corpus-restricted atrophic gastritis, including serological markers (anti-parietal-cell and anti-intrinsic-factor antibodies, chromogranin A), histopathological staging, and endoscopic surveillance protocols
topic.why_matters: Autoimmune gastritis is underdiagnosed yet carries significant risks including pernicious anemia, iron deficiency, ECL cell hyperplasia, and progression to gastric carcinoid tumors (type I NETs), requiring structured surveillance

characteristics.what_it_measures: Selective destruction of oxyntic mucosa by autoimmune mechanisms
characteristics.value: Atrophy restricted to gastric corpus/fundus with antral sparing
characteristics.vs_baseline: Contrasts with H. pylori gastritis which typically begins in the antrum
characteristics.context: Histopathological hallmark distinguishing autoimmune from other forms of chronic gastritis

good_for: Understanding pathophysiology of autoimmune gastric atrophy
Guiding surveillance for ECL hyperplasia and type I gastric carcinoids
Differential diagnosis of corpus-predominant atrophic gastritis
requires: Histopathological assessment with corpus and antral biopsies
Serological panel: anti-parietal-cell antibodies, anti-intrinsic-factor antibodies, gastrin, chromogranin A

novelty: Consolidates fragmented literature on autoimmune gastritis into a clinically actionable review
Clarifies the ECL hyperplasia → carcinoid progression pathway and surveillance rationale
open_problems: Optimal surveillance intervals for ECL hyperplasia remain undefined
No validated biomarker panel for early autoimmune gastritis detection before overt atrophy
Role of H. pylori co-infection in modifying autoimmune gastritis natural history is unclear
